:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 4,335 people who were eligible for parole but not released from prison, constituting 19% of the parole-eligible prison population.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

Offenders convicted of class C or D felonies related to drugs, DWI, or other non- violent crimes are generally eligible for release after 15% of their sentence has been served. Offenders convicted of class A or B non-violent or drug felonies, or class B DWIs are eligible for release after 25% of their sentence has been served. If a parolee is serving consecutive sentences, the minimum term for eligibility for parole must be calculated by adding the minimum terms for each individual sentence together; however, this must not exceed the minimum term for parole eligibility for an ordinary life sentence. Several types of convictions make offenders ineligible for parole; these include convictions for first-degree murder, tampering with a victim or witness, certain recidivist drug trafficking offenses, and conviction as a persis- tent sexual offender. Circuit courts have jurisdiction to grant parole to offenders serving sentences under 2 1/2 years (in other words, those serving non-prison sentences). There do not appear to be guidelines or other criteria for court-based parole release.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison, accounting for 69% (2,635 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ### Age ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Overview ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, there were 1,650 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 38% of the parole-eligible prison population.

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.
